Commercial Apr 7, 2026

Iran War Fallout: Airlines Slash Thousands Of Flights Amid Global Fuel Shortages

As the Iran crisis escalates, airlines worldwide are grappling with skyrocketing fuel costs and limited access to jet fuel.

Geopolitical disruptions are forcing airlines to reduce capacity rather than absorb margin-crushing fuel cost increases, signaling potential industry-wide consolidation if tensions persist.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Apr 7, 2026

I’m A Flight Attendant: This Is How We Handle Difficult Passengers

Always trying to resolve issues before they start.

Flight attendants prioritize de-escalation and environmental management to prevent passenger incidents rather than reactive enforcement, reducing operational disruptions and safety risks.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 7, 2026

Drought Parches Florida

The state was unusually dry for much of 2025, but the intensity of the drought has ratcheted up since January 2026.

Prolonged drought conditions in Florida may impact aviation operations through reduced water availability for aircraft cooling systems and fire suppression infrastructure at regional airports.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 7, 2026

Artemis 2 swings around the moon

Four astronauts from the United States and Canada became the humans to travel the furthest from the Earth April 6 as they went around the moon on the Artemis 2 mission.

Artemis 2's successful lunar flyby demonstrates NASA's readiness for crewed moon landing operations and validates the SLS/Orion system before the Artemis 3 surface mission.

See SpaceNews →

PAI
Regulatory Apr 7, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

SMS Declaration of Compliance demonstrates FAA's shift toward outcome-based regulation, requiring operators to self-certify their safety management systems rather than relying solely on agency audits.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 7, 2026

The Problem With Ultra Long-Haul Flights Airlines Don’t Talk About

Why Air New Zealand is unable to compete on its flagship route.

Ultra long-haul routes require massive fuel loads that compromise payload capacity, forcing airlines to choose between distance and profitability.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 7, 2026

5 Fighter Jets With The Most Advanced Radar Systems

The fighters with the longest striking range.

Modern fighter radar capabilities now extend engagement ranges beyond 200 nautical miles, fundamentally changing air combat tactics and defensive requirements.

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

Air Canada Expands To The Canary Islands & Latin America, Thanks To The Airbus A321XLR & A220

The carrier is making big moves in high-yield markets.

Air Canada's A321XLR and A220 deployment enables profitable ultra-long-range point-to-point service to previously underserved markets without hub dependency.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

Here's How Air Canada's Airbus A321XLR 1-1 Lie-Flat Suites Compare To Widebody Business Class

The A321XLR's 4,700-nautical-mile range opens secondary European cities but does the 12.2-foot-wide cabin match the widebody experience?

Air Canada's A321XLR cabin width constraint (12.2 feet vs. typical widebody 20+ feet) requires creative suite design that may compromise the spacious lie-flat experience passengers expect in premium long-haul cabins.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Apr 7, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective SRM requires organizations to systematically implement layered controls while explicitly documenting why remaining risks are operationally acceptable.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

“There’s A Huge Demand”: The Boeing 787-8 Dreamliner Demolished After Only 13 Flying Hours

A barely flown Boeing 787-8 is scrapped because its parts are worth more than the whole.

Supply chain constraints and parts scarcity are now driving aircraft parting economics, signaling wider structural issues in aerospace manufacturing recovery.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

The Cost Of Flying Premium Economy In 2026 Compared To Economy

The hidden truth about premium economy.

Premium economy pricing strategies in 2026 reveal carriers are leveraging capacity constraints and ancillary revenue models to compress traditional cabin-class distinctions.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

The World's Largest Airline By Number Of Hubs

American Airlines reigns supreme on this front.

American Airlines' hub dominance creates significant operational complexity and regulatory scrutiny around network resilience and competitive concentration in U.S. aviation.

See Simple Flying →

Drones Apr 6, 2026

China tests heavy cargo drone designed for extreme terrain

China has completed the maiden flight of a new 7-tonne autonomous cargo aircraft, taking a...

China's 7-tonne autonomous cargo drone addresses logistics gaps in mountainous regions where traditional aviation infrastructure is limited or nonexistent.

See sUAS News →

▶20:47
Regulatory Apr 7, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Policy & Safety Promotion

This video provides an overview of implementing a safety management system (SMS), specifically aligned with FAA Part 5 regulations. Join us as we explore Blue Skies Charter, a fictional company, and learn about general SMS requirements, safety policy development and safety promotion strategies. This video covers essential components like leadership commitment, gap analysis, safety objectives, emergency response planning and employee training. Whether you're a small charter operator or interested in aviation safety, this video offers valuable insights into building an effective SMS.

Small operators can implement SMS compliance cost-effectively by adopting Blue Skies Charter's framework, which prioritizes leadership commitment and employee training as foundational elements before addressing complex gap analysis.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

Not So Fast: How The Boeing 757 Made A Comeback At This Luxury Airline

The aircraft is surprisingly rejoining the airline's fleet.

The 757's resurgence signals renewed operator confidence in the aircraft's efficiency and economics despite its production discontinuation two decades ago.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

NASA’s Artemis II Crew Eclipses Record for Farthest Human Spaceflight

Editor’s note: This release was updated at 7:28 p.m. EDT on April 6, 2026, to reflect Orion’s final closest approach to the lunar surface from about 4,070 miles to about 4,067 miles. NASA also updated the farthest distance Orion will travel. Finally, NASA also made changes at 10 p.m. to the...

Artemis II's trajectory breaks human spaceflight distance records, validating Orion capsule systems for eventual lunar landing missions and demonstrating NASA's deep-space navigation precision.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

NASA’s Northrop Grumman CRS-24 Mission Overview

NASA’s Northrop Grumman Commercial Resupply Services 24 mission, or Northrop Grumman CRS-24, will deliver approximately 11,000 pounds of science and supplies to the International Space Station. This mission will be the second flight of the Cygnus XL, the larger, more cargo-capable version of the...

The Cygnus XL variant increases ISS resupply capacity by approximately 27% compared to standard Cygnus, enabling NASA to deliver more science experiments and maintenance supplies per mission.

See NASA →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

K-State Salina Launches High School Aviation Camp

Weeklong program will include simulator work, ground school and up to three hours of flight time.

Hands-on aviation education programs like this bridge the critical pilot shortage by exposing high school students to realistic flying experience before college commitment.

See AVweb →

▶15:03
Safety Apr 7, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Assurance

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety assurance, which evaluates the continued effectiveness of implemented risk control strategies and supports the identification of new hazards. Whether you're an owner, operator or part of a small aviation team, this guide provides valuable insights and tools to enhance your safety management practices and ensure a safer operational environment.

Safety assurance in SMS requires continuous evaluation of risk controls and proactive hazard identification, not just reactive compliance with regulations.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

Moog Highlights Growing Satellite Bus Capabilities with Full‑Scale METEOR Reveal at Space Symposium

East Aurora, NY — Moog Inc. (NYSE: MOG.A and MOG.B), a worldwide designer, manufacturer and systems integrator of high-performance precision motion and fluid controls and control systems will highlight its […]...

Moog's METEOR platform expansion signals increased competition in satellite bus manufacturing, potentially reshaping supply chains for aerospace prime contractors.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

FOSSA targets Japan’s defense market as larger smallsats expand capabilities

Spanish startup FOSSA Systems is pushing into Japan’s defense market after securing a local partner to expand its reach, building on a shift beyond tiny picosatellites used to connect low-power monitoring devices toward more capable spacecraft for broader government applications.

FOSSA's evolution from picosatellites to defense-capable platforms signals how commercial smallsat providers are capturing government contracts by expanding technical capabilities beyond IoT applications.

See SpaceNews →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

Air Tractor Holdings Acquires Thrush Aircraft

Deal combines two ag aviation manufacturers under shared ownership.

Agricultural aviation consolidation reduces competition among specialized airframe manufacturers, potentially affecting pricing and innovation in the niche spray-plane market.

See AVweb →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

Hartzell Expands Top Prop Program

Update adds 150+ models, pricing changes for GA aircraft upgrades.

Hartzell's expansion of its top prop upgrade program to 150+ GA models signals growing aftermarket demand for performance improvements as older aircraft remain economically viable.

See AVweb →

Industry Apr 7, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational efficiency improvements across airline and airport systems offer a near-term pathway to reduce aviation's environmental footprint without waiting for technological breakthroughs.

See MDPI →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

Poll: Will Phoenix Trigger Broader Landing Fees?

Could Phoenix-area landing fees spark broader GA change?

Phoenix's landing fee implementation may pressure other regional airports to adopt similar revenue models, potentially fragmenting GA cost structures nationwide.

See AVweb →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

Landing Fees and Flight Training in Phoenix

As Falcon Field and Mesa Gateway add landing fees for light aircraft, officials and flight schools describe different futures for the Phoenix area.

Rising landing fees at Phoenix's two primary training airports could consolidate flight schools into fewer facilities, potentially reducing training capacity and competition in a key regional aviation market.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

Night Sky Network Celebrates Artemis II

Today, the crew of Artemis II reaches a milestone, traveling farther than any humans in recorded history, as they orbit the far side of the Moon, roughly 4,000 miles above the lunar surface. You can watch this historic broadcast on NASA’s YouTube channel today, April 6, 2026, at 10 AM Pacific (1 PM...

Artemis II's lunar orbit achievement demonstrates NASA's sustained capability to execute complex deep-space missions, validating technologies and procedures essential for future human Moon landings.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

Grants

Grants Status Requests To submit a request, visit NASA General Information Request Form and complete the form. You will receive an automated email with the most commonly requested grant status information. Important Instructions: How to Fill Out the Form: Memorandum for NASA Grantee Community...

NASA's streamlined grant status request process now delivers automated responses, reducing administrative burden for aerospace research organizations managing federal funding.

See NASA →

Military Apr 6, 2026

Lady of Larissa Flies Again: Incredible New Footage Emerges of the Secretive RQ-180 Spy Drone

New videos give an even clearer view – unquestionably our best ever – of the legendary classified stealth drone, previously seen landing in Greece last month. Last month, we reported on images taken in Larissa, Greece, showing what we, and others, concluded – based on prior sightings and comment...

High-resolution footage of the RQ-180 stealth drone strengthens the case for the aircraft's operational status and suggests increased flight testing activities beyond classified military operations.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

The Near Side of the Moon

The astronauts aboard NASA’s Orion spacecraft captured this photo of the Moon’s near side on April 4, 2026. The image features dark patches at center and right; these are ancient lava flows, which are unique to this side of the Moon. The Artemis II astronauts – astronauts Victor Glover, Reid...

Artemis II's lunar imagery documents the Moon's near-side geological features, providing essential visual data for future crewed landing site assessment and mission planning.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

Amazon Leo to serve as launchpad for more Delta brand experiences

The proverbial ‘gravitational pull’ of low-latency Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite-powered inflight connectivity is continuing unabated in commercial aviation, as Delta Air Lines earmarks Amazon Leo for an initial 500 tails from 2028. Announced on 31 March, the arrangement springboards off the US...

Delta's commitment to Amazon's Kuiper LEO satellites signals that low-latency inflight connectivity will become a competitive differentiator, with 500-aircraft deployment by 2028 establishing new service quality standards across the industry.

See Runway Girl Network →

Military Apr 6, 2026

USAF F-16s Flying With AGM-158 JASSMs in Operation Epic Fury Against Iran

Long-range JASSM strikes still continue on Iranian targets, particularly against missile and drone production facilities. U.S. Air Force F-16s currently deployed in support of Operation Epic Fury have been shown in official photos carrying the AGM-158 Joint-Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile (JASSM)...

Extended-range standoff munitions enable F-16s to strike high-value targets from safer distances, reducing aircraft exposure to advanced air defenses.

See The Aviationist →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

National STOL Announces 2026 Competition Schedule

Series plans eight events across the U.S. beginning in April.

National STOL's expansion to eight events signals growing demand for short-runway operations training and competition among bush pilots and backcountry operators.

See AVweb →

Drones Apr 6, 2026

Invisible drone defense helped secure the massive JUNO Awards crowd

When tens of thousands of fans gather under one roof, security doesn’t just stop at the doors; it extends to the skies above. That was exactly the case at Canada’s biggest night in music, the JUNO Awards, where a quiet but critical layer of anti-drone protection was keeping watch overhead. more…...

Event venues now require invisible airspace security systems to protect large crowds, creating a new specialized market segment for counter-UAS providers.

See DroneDJ →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

The moon base has a hardware plan. It needs a software strategy, too.

Early in my career at SpaceX, I was the only person dedicated to training the mission control team for Dragon. For three years, I built the training for the operations team. The documentation came second, always the thing I’d get to later, always the thing that felt like overhead against the actual...

Lunar infrastructure development requires equally rigorous software operations planning and mission control training as hardware engineering to ensure sustainable long-term functionality.

See SpaceNews →
